focus: Input validation, information disclosure, missing middleware, plugin security

findings.title: No Zod validation on any API route request bodies
findings.description: Zod schemas exist in src/core/types.ts (CreateTaskInput, UpdateTaskInput, CreateSectionInput, CreateTemplateInput) but are NEVER called with .parse() or .safeParse() in any API route handler. All routes do `await c.req.json()` and pass raw unvalidated bodies directly to service methods. The TypeScript type annotation on the service method signature is erased at runtime, so arbitrary fields and types flow through unchecked.
findings.recommendation: Add Zod .parse() or .safeParse() calls at the API boundary for every POST/PUT/PATCH body. Example: `const input = CreateTaskInput.parse(await c.req.json())`

findings.title: No global body-size limit middleware
findings.description: The Hono server has no body-limit middleware. Only the voice transcribe route has a per-route size check via Content-Length header. All other JSON endpoints accept arbitrarily large request bodies, enabling memory exhaustion attacks. A single POST to /api/tasks with a multi-GB JSON body would consume all server memory.
findings.recommendation: Add `import { bodyLimit } from 'hono/body-limit'` and apply globally: `app.use('*', bodyLimit({ maxSize: 10 * 1024 * 1024 }))`. Override to 25MB for voice routes only.

findings.title: Plugin sandbox is a no-op — plugins run in main process with full access
findings.description: src/plugins/sandbox.ts createSandbox() returns an empty stub. Plugins are loaded via raw `import()` in the main Node.js process (src/plugins/loader.ts:250). A malicious community plugin has unrestricted access to the filesystem, network, process, and all service objects. Permission checks in createPluginAPI() only restrict the injected API object — the plugin code itself can import any Node.js module.
findings.recommendation: Until real sandboxing is implemented (vm module, Worker, or separate process), add a prominent warning in the UI when installing community plugins. Consider using Node.js --experimental-permission flags or worker_threads with restricted permissions.

findings.title: Task import endpoint has no array length limit
findings.description: POST /api/tasks/import accepts a body with `{ tasks: [...] }` but never validates the array length. An attacker can send millions of task objects, causing the server to loop indefinitely creating tasks. Bulk complete/delete/update have a 500-item limit but import does not.
findings.recommendation: Add validation: `if (!Array.isArray(importedTasks) || importedTasks.length > 1000) return c.json({ error: '...' }, 400)`

findings.title: Task reorder endpoint accepts unbounded array without validation
findings.description: POST /api/tasks/reorder accepts `{ orderedIds }` with no type check or length limit. Sending a non-array or a massive array will cause undefined behavior or memory issues.
findings.recommendation: Validate that orderedIds is an array of strings with a reasonable length limit.

findings.title: Section reorder endpoint accepts unbounded array without validation
findings.description: POST /api/sections/reorder accepts `{ orderedIds }` with no type or length validation, same issue as task reorder.
findings.recommendation: Validate orderedIds is an array of strings with a reasonable length limit.

findings.title: Comment content has no length validation
findings.description: POST /api/tasks/:id/comments and PATCH /api/comments/:id accept `body.content` cast as string with no length limit. An attacker can store arbitrarily large comment text in the database.
findings.recommendation: Add max length validation for comment content (e.g., 50,000 characters).

findings.title: AI chat message has no length validation
findings.description: POST /api/ai/chat only checks `if (!message)` but does not limit the message length. An extremely long message could consume excessive LLM API tokens or cause memory issues during context assembly.
findings.recommendation: Add a reasonable max length (e.g., 32,000 characters) for chat messages.

findings.title: AI session title has no length validation
findings.description: PUT /api/ai/sessions/:id/title accepts any length title string without validation.
findings.recommendation: Limit title to a reasonable length (e.g., 500 characters).

findings.title: AI memory content and category have no validation
findings.description: PUT /api/ai/memories/:id accepts content and category from the request body without validating that category is one of the expected enum values or that content has a reasonable length.
findings.recommendation: Validate category against the known enum values and add a max length for content.

findings.title: Project create/update accepts arbitrary fields passed to service
findings.description: POST /api/projects and PATCH /api/projects/:id pass raw body fields to the project service without validation. Fields like `color`, `icon`, `viewStyle`, and `parentId` are not validated against expected types or value ranges. A malicious `viewStyle` value outside 'list'|'board'|'calendar' could cause UI issues.
findings.recommendation: Create a Zod schema for project create/update input and validate at the API boundary.

findings.title: Tag create accepts any name/color without validation
findings.description: POST /api/tags passes body.name and body.color directly to tagService.create() without type checking or length limits. An extremely long tag name or invalid color value could be stored.
findings.recommendation: Validate tag name (min 1, max 100 chars) and color (valid hex/CSS color).

findings.title: Timeblocking RPC endpoint passes unvalidated args to store methods
findings.description: POST /api/plugins/timeblocking/rpc accepts a `method` string and `args` array, then dispatches to the store. While the method names are whitelisted via a switch statement, the args are passed directly without validation. Malformed args (wrong types, oversized strings, extra properties) flow through unchecked.
findings.recommendation: Add per-method argument validation, especially for createBlock and updateBlock which accept object arguments.

findings.title: Plugin install URL private network check is incomplete for 172.x ranges
findings.description: The private network check in POST /api/plugins/install blocks all hostnames starting with '172.' but RFC 1918 private range is only 172.16.0.0-172.31.255.255. More importantly, the check does not block IPv6 private addresses (fc00::/7, fe80::/10), DNS rebinding attacks (hostname resolves to private IP after initial URL parse), or 0.0.0.0.
findings.recommendation: Block 0.0.0.0, ::, IPv6 private ranges (fc00::/7, fe80::/10). Consider using DNS resolution before fetching to catch DNS rebinding. Also narrow 172.x to 172.16-31.x specifically.

findings.title: Voice proxy forwards raw external API error text to client
findings.description: The voice proxy endpoints (synthesize, inworld-synthesize, inworld-voices) forward raw error text from external APIs directly to the client. This could leak internal API details, rate limit information, or account-specific error messages from Groq/Inworld.
findings.recommendation: Return generic error messages to the client. Log the detailed error server-side only.

findings.title: AI chat SSE streams raw error messages to client on catch
findings.description: The /api/ai/chat endpoint catches errors and streams err.message directly to the client via SSE. Internal errors (database failures, provider initialization issues) could leak implementation details.
findings.recommendation: For non-AI-provider errors, return a generic message. Only forward provider-specific errors (auth, rate limit) that are meaningful to the user.

findings.title: Settings GET endpoint allows reading any setting key including sensitive ones
findings.description: GET /api/settings/:key has no allowlist — it can read any app setting including ai_api_key and ai_oauth_token values. While PUT has a WRITABLE_SETTING_KEYS allowlist, GET does not have a corresponding READABLE_SETTING_KEYS restriction. The /api/ai/config endpoint already properly returns `hasApiKey: !!value` instead of the actual key, but the settings endpoint bypasses this.
findings.recommendation: Add a SENSITIVE_KEYS set and either block reads of those keys or redact the values (return `hasValue: true` instead).

findings.title: JSON.parse of untrusted toolCalls from database
findings.description: In /api/ai/sessions/:id/switch, row.toolCalls is JSON.parse'd from the database and spread into message objects. If the database was tampered with (or a bug stored malicious JSON), this could introduce unexpected properties including __proto__.
findings.recommendation: Use a safe JSON parse that rejects __proto__ keys, or validate the parsed structure before spreading.

findings.title: No rate limiting on any API endpoint
findings.description: There is no rate limiting middleware on any endpoint. The AI chat endpoint is particularly vulnerable — each request triggers an LLM API call that may cost money and take significant time. An attacker with local network access could rapidly drain API credits.
findings.recommendation: Add rate limiting middleware, at minimum on POST /api/ai/chat (e.g., 30 requests per minute). Consider using hono/rate-limiter or a simple in-memory token bucket.

findings.title: Task relation type not validated against enum
findings.description: POST /api/tasks/:id/relations passes `body.type ?? 'blocks'` directly to addRelation without validating that type is one of the expected values. While the default 'blocks' is safe, any string could be stored as the relation type.
findings.recommendation: Validate that body.type is 'blocks' (or whatever valid relation types exist) before passing to the service.

summary.key_theme: The primary systemic issue is that Zod schemas exist but are never used at the API boundary. Every POST/PUT/PATCH handler passes raw JSON directly to service methods. This means all the carefully defined type constraints (max lengths, enum values, integer ranges) in src/core/types.ts are compile-time-only and provide zero runtime protection. Adding Zod .parse() calls at the API boundary would address findings SEC-R3-01 and partially mitigate SEC-R3-04 through SEC-R3-13.
